content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 ,I Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Marcia L. Scully 
General Counsel 
June 22, 2016 
Metropolitan Waler District of Southern California 
Box 54153 
Los Angeles. CA 90054-0153 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-086 
Dear Ms. Scully: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Yen C. Tu, a member of the 
Board of Directors of the Metropolitan Water District of Southern California ("MWDSC"), 
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 
1090 et seq. 1 Please note that we do not advise on any other area oflaw, including Public Contract 
Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice 
(In re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete 
and accurate. 
When a request for advice involves potential issues raised under Section I 090, the 
Commission is required to forward a copy of the request to the Attorney General's Office and the 
local district attorney prior to proceeding with the advice. (Section 1097 .1 ( c)(3 ). ) Accordingly, we 
have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and the Los Angeles County District 
Attorney's Office. We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) 
Finally, the following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any 
individual other than the requester. (Section 1097.1 (c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS 
1. Does the Section 87407 prohibit Director Tu from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to MWH Americas, Inc. ("MWH"), a consulting, engineering, and construction firm that 
specializes in infrastructure and water projects, with whom Director Tu's own consulting business 
is currently negotiating prospective employment? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit Director Tu' s consulting business from entering into a 
contract to provide outreach and public education services as a subcontractor to MWH (the 
"Subcontract") on MWH's contract with the City of San Diego to manage the City's Pure Water 
Program (the ''City Contract")? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 910l4. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 oflhe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-086 
Page No. 2 
3. If Director Tu's consulting business enters into Subcontract with MWH, would Section 
I 090 prohibit her from making or participating in the making of, or MWDSC from entering into, 
future contracts with MWH? 
CONCLUSIONS 
I. Yes. Section 87407 prohibits Director Tu from taking part in any decision directly 
relating to MWH because she is negotiating prospective employment with MWH. 
2. No. Section I 090 does not prohibit Director Tu's consulting business from entering into 
the Subcontract. 
3. Yes. If Director Tu's consulting business enters into the Subcontract, Section 1090 would 
prohibit her from making or participating in the making of, and MWDSC from entering into, future 
contracts with MWH because she would be financially interested in those future contracts. 
FACTS 
You are the General Counsel of the MWDSC and the authorized representative of Director 
Yen C. Tu. The Mayor of the City of San Diego, with the approval of the City Council, appointed 
Director Tu to represent the City on the San Diego County Water Authority ( .. SD8WA") Board of 
Directors pursuant to the San Diego County Water Authority Act. In her capacity as a SDCWA 
Director, Director Tu represents SDCWA on the MWDSC Board of Directors. Director Tu's role 
with the City does not include the administration, review, or approval of contracts entered into by 
the City. 
Last year, the City awarded a contract to manage its Pure Water Program to MWH, a 
consulting, engineering, and construction finn that specializes in infrastructure and water projects, 
and Brown and Caldwell, another consulting finn. As part of their contract with the City, MWH and 
Brown and Caldwell agreed to provide outreach and public education services for the project. 
Director Tu owns a consulting business in San Diego that specializes in marketing, 
community outreach, and conducting special events. Director Tu 's consulting business is currently 
negotiating the Subcontract with MWH to provide outreach and public education services on behalf 
of MWH on the City Contract. Director Tu's consulting fee under the Subcontract would be capped 
at $20,000 annually. The initial tenn of the Subcontract would end at the conclusion of the 2016 
calendar year, but the Subcontract would be renewable for four additional years for up to five years 
total. Director Tu has no ownership or other financial interest in MWH and would not participate in 
any profit sharing or receive financial benefits from the finn other than payments pursuant to the 
Subcontract. 
MWH is one of only eight firms of its type currently offering services related to MWDSC's 
mission and has a long history of contracting with MWDSC. MWH currently has seven active 
contracts for engineering, design, planning, and related services with MWDSC. Although there are 
no pending amendments to the current contracts or new contracts, existing contracts may be 
modified, and MWDSC will continue to have the opportunity and need to enter into new contracts 


File No. A-16-086 
Page No. 3 
with MWH, in the foreseeable future. According to its website, MWH is comprised of 
approximately 7,000 contractors and employees. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87407 
We first consider whether Section 87407 prohibits Director Tu from taking part in any 
governmental decision directly relating to MWH. Section 87407 is designed to ensure that a public 
official does not use his or her position to make any governmental decisions that unduly benefit a 
prospective private sector employer. Section 87407 provides as follows: 
"No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her 
official position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to 
any person with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement 
concerning, prospective employment." 
Thus, once a public official has negotiated prospective employment or has made an arrangement 
regarding prospective employment, Section 87407 prohibits the official from making, participating 
in making, or using his or her official position to influence any governmental decision directly 
relating to the prospective employer.2 
For purposes of Section 87407, an official is "negotiating" employment when he or she 
interviews or discusses an offer of employment with an employer; has an "arrangement" concerning 
prospective employment when he or she accepts an employer's offer of employment; and is not 
"negotiating" or does not have an "arrangement" concerning prospective employment ifhe or she 
rejects or is rejected for employment. (Regulation 1874 7( c)( 1 )-(3 ).) 
Director Tu is negotiating prospective employment with MWH because she has discussed 
the Subcontract, an offer for contract employment with MWH. She would have an arrangement 
concerning prospective employment with MWH if she accepts MWH's offer and enters into the 
Subcontract. However, she would not be negotiating prospective employment with MWH if she 
rejects, or MWH retracts its offer of, the Subcontract. (Regulation 1874 7( c)(3 ).) 
Because Director Tu is negotiating prospective contract employment with MWH, Section 
87407 prohibits her from taking part in any decision directly relating to MWH, unless she rejects or 
is rejected for the contract employment with MWH. 
2 You have also asked whether prospective contract employment constitutes "prospective employment" for 
purposes of Section 87407. We have previously advised that Section 87407 prohibits a public official that negotiates 
contract work with a prospective employer from taking pan in any governmental decision directly relating to the 
prospective employer. (Culp Advice Lener, No. I-14-051; Hildreth Advice Letter, No.A-11-203; see Rctbamacl Advice 
Lener, No. A-I 0-030.) Therefore, consistent with our previous ad\'ice, we conclude that prospective contract 
employment constitutes "prospective employment" for purposes of Section 87407. 


Section 10903 
File No. A- 16-086 
Page No. 4 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities from 
making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with financial 
interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising 
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigal v. 
City of Taft ( 1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual 
impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey 
(1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig ( 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the tenns of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.) When an officer with a proscribed financial interest is a member of the governing body of a 
public entity, the prohibition of Section I 090 extends to the entire body. (89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49, 
50 (2006); see Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. CozmǬv of Del Norte ( 1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201, 2 11-
212.) 
We employ the following analysis to determine whether an official has a conflict of interest 
under Section 1090. 
Step One: Is Director Tu subject to the provisions of Section 1090? 
Section I 090 provides in pertinent part as follows: "Members of the Legislature, state, 
county, district, judicial district and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in 
any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by anybody or board of which they are 
members." 
Director Tu is appointed by the Mayor of the City of San Diego with City Council approval 
to serve on the SDCW A Board, and in her capacity as a SDCW A Director, she represents SDCW A 
on the MWDSC Board. Director Tu's duties in her role as a City representative to the SDCW A 
Board do not include the administration, review, or approval of contracts entered into by the City. 
In each of these capacities with each of these agencies, the director is a public official for purposes 
of Section 1090. Therefore, Director Tu is subject to Section 1090. 
iWe do not analyze whether Section 87100 of the Act would prohibit Director Tu from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to future contracts with MWH if her consulting business enters into the Subcontract 
because Section 1090 would apply and would prohibit her from making or participating in the making of those future 
contracts. Note that Section 87100 would also prohibit Director Tu from making, participating in making, or using her 
official position to influence a governmental decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on her financial interest in MWH as a source of income 1f Section I 090 is not applicable. 


File No. A-16-086 
Page No. 5 
Steps Two and Three: Do the decisions at issue involve a contract that Director Tu will make 
in her official capacity? 
The Subcontract 
The decision at issue is whether MWH should enter into the Subcontract with Director Tu's 
consulting business. 
The Subcontract is between two private entities, MWH and Director Tu's consulting 
business. Furthennore, there is no indication from the facts presented that Director Tu "made or 
participated in the making" of the Subcontract in her capacity as a public official, or that she 
exerted any improper influence over the fonnation of the Subcontract in any way. Because Director 
Tu did not "make or participate in the making" of the Subcontract in her official capacity, we 
conclude that Section 1090 does not prohibit Director Tu's consulting business from entering into 
the Subcontract and do not further analyze this contract. 
Future Contracts with MWH 
Does the decision at issue involve a contract? 
The decision at issue is whether MWDSC ·may enter into potential future contracts with 
MWH if Director Tu' s consulting business enters into the Subcontract. 
To detennine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 34, 36 (2011 ); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 
( 1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and l 097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
'contract."' (Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569-571.) A decision to modify, 
extend, or renegotiate a contract constitutes involvement in the making of a contract under Section 
l 090. ( City of Imperial Beach, supra, at pp. 167-197.) 
MWH currently has seven active contracts for engineering, design, planning, and related 
services with MWDSC. Although there are no pending amendments to the current contracts or new 
contracts, you state that those existing contracts may be amended or modified, and MWDSC will 
continue to have the opportunity and need to enter into new contracts with MWH, in the foreseeable 
future. Thus, a decision relating to MWDSC's adoption of a new contract with MWH or its 
modification, extension, or renegotiation of an existing contract between them would involve a 
contract within the meaning of Section l 090. 
Would Director Tu be making or participating in the making of the potential future contracts 
between MWDSC and MWH? 
Where an official is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the 
contract at issue, he or she is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her 
agency's contracts irrespective of whether he or she actually participates in the making of the 


File No. A-16-086 
Page No. 6 
contract. (Thomson, supra, 38 Cal.3d 633, 649; Stigall, supra, 58 Cal.2d 565, 570-571; Fraser­
Yamor Agency, Inc. , .. County of Del Norte, supra. at pp. 21 1-212; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 49 (2006).) 
Thus, Director Tu would be presumed to be involved in the making of any potential future 
contract between MWDSC and MWH entered into while she is a member of the MWDSC Board 
because she is a member of the MWDSC Board. 
Step Four: \Vould Director Tu have a financial interest in potential future contracts between 
M\VDSC and M\VH? 
You assert that Director Tu would not be "financially interested" in potential future 
contracts between MWDSC and MWH if her consulting business enters into the potential 
Subcontract with MWH pursuant to Eden Township Healthcare Dist. v. Sutter Health (201 1 )  202 
Cal.App.4th 208. We view the holding from Eden more narrowly. 
In Eden, a district official participated in negotiating public contracts with a nonprofit 
corporation that employed the official as its president and chief executive officer. (Id. at pp. 2 1 2-
214.) The official had no ownership interest in the nonprofit corporation. (Ibid.) The appellate court 
found that the official was not financially interested those contracts because there was no evidence 
to suggest that the official's compensation would in any way be impacted by those contracts. (Id. at 
p. 227.) 
The facts presented are distinguishable from those in Eden. Director Tu is not a salaried 
employee of a nonprofit organization, she is the principal of her own for-profit consulting firm. The 
potential Subcontract between MWH and Director Tu's consulting business would confer a direct 
personal benefit to Director Tu, her annual consulting fee of up to $20,000, and would be renewable 
for four additional years beyond its initial one-year term for up to five years total. Director Tu's 
ongoing compensation from MWH under the Subcontract could be impacted by the potential future 
contracts between MWDSC and MWH because MWH could decide not to renew the Subcontract if 
Director Tu does not effectively advocate for MWH in her role as a MWDSC Director with respect 
to the potential future contracts. Therefore, Eden is not applicable to the facts presented. 
Section l 090 attempts to prevent honest government agents from succumbing to temptation 
by making it illegal for them to enter into relationships which are fraught with temptation. 
(Thomson, supra, at p. 648 citing Stigall, supra, at p. 570.) "The defining characteristic of a 
prohibited financial interest is whether it has the potential to divide an official's loyalties and 
compromise the undivided representation of the public interests the official is charged with 
protecting." (Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1075.) 
If Director Tu's consulting business enters into the Subcontract, she will be employed by 
MWH, and her ongoing employment under the Subcontract beyond its initial one-year term would 
be dependent on MWH's renewal of the Subcontract. In other words, in order to ensure the renewal 
of the Subcontract, Director Tu would be reliant on MWH. Accordingly, we find that Director Tu 
would be financially interested for purposes of Section 1090 in the potential future contracts 
between MWDSC and MWH if her consulting business enters into the Subcontract. 


Step Five: Does either a remote interest or non-interest exception apply? 
File No. A-16-086 
Page No. 7 
The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1 090's prohibition where 
the financial interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a 
"noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. 
If a "remote interest'' is present, the contract may be made if ( 1) the officer in question 
discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the body or board of which the officer is a 
member, (2) the interest is noted in the body or board's official records, and (3) the officer abstains 
from any participation in the body or board's making of the contract. (Section I 091 (a); 88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 1 06, 1 08 (2005); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).) If a "noninterest" is 
present, the contract may be made without the officer's abstention, and generally, a noninterest does 
not require disclosure. (City of Vernon v. Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 
508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (200 I ).) 
Reviewing the statutory exceptions, it appears that three "remote interest" exceptions, 
identified below, may apply to Director Tu. However, it does not appear that any of the nonintercst 
exceptions apply to Director Tu. 
We now consider the applicability of these remote interest exceptions to the facts presented. 
Section I 09 l(b)(2) 
The first exception under consideration is found in Section 1 09l (b)(2), which provides that 
there is a "remote interest" when: (I) the private contracting party has IO  or more employees other 
than the officer; (2) the officer was employed by the private contracting party at least three years 
prior to initially joining the public body; (3) the officer owns less than 3% of the stock in the private 
contracting party; (4) the officer is not an officer or director of the private contracting party; and (5) 
the officer did not directly participate in formulating the bid of the private contracting party. 
Based on the facts presented, Director Tu has not been employed by MWH for at least three 
years prior to initially joining the MWDSC Board. Therefore, this remote interest exception is not 
applicable. 
Section I 091 (b )(3) 
The exception set forth in Section 1091 (b )(3) provides that there is a "remote interest" 
when: ( 1) the agency with which the officer is a local public agency located in a county with a 
population ofless than 4,000,000; (2) the contract is competitively bid and is not for personal 
services; (3) the officer is not in a primary management capacity of the contracting party and holds 
no ownership interest in the contracting party; ( 4) the contracting party has IO or more other 
employees; (5) the employee or agent did not directly participate in formulating the bid of the 
contracting party; and (6) the contracting party is the lowest responsible bidder. 
MWDSC's headquarters is located in the City of Los Angeles within the County of Los 
Angeles, and the County's population is in excess of 10,000,000. Moreover, the potential 
Subcontract between MWH and Director Tu's consulting business is for the provision of outreach 


File No. A-16-086 
Page No. 8 
and public education services for the City of San Diego's Pure Water Program, and these services 
are personal services. Therefore, this remote interest does not apply. 
Section 1 09 l(b )(8) 
The third exception, contained in Section I 091 (b )(8), provides that there is a "remote 
interest" when the public officer has been a supplier of goods or services to the contracting party for 
at least five years prior to his or her election or appointment to office. 
Director Tu has not been employed by MWH for at least five years prior to initially joining 
the MWDSC Board. Therefore, this remote interest is not applicable. 
Step Six: Docs the rule of necessity apply'! 
In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a 
contract that Section I 090 would otherwise prohibit. (88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 1 10 (2005).) In 
procurement situations, the rule of necessity has permitted a government agency to acquire an 
essential supply or service despite a conflict of interest when the contracting officer would be the 
sole source of supply of the essential supply or service available. (65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 3 1 0  
( 1 982).) The rule of necessity has been found applicable only "in extreme cases of emergency, or 
where no alternative source of supply of goods or services existed." (59 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 604, 6 1 9, 
fn. 18 (1976).) 
You have provided no facts to suggest the "rule of necessity" would apply in the present 
situation. 
Thus, we conclude that if Director Tu' s consulting business enters into the Subcontract with 
MWH, Section 1090 would prohibit her from making or participating in the making of, and 
MWDSC from entering into, potential future contracts with MWH. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel
- 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
1. Does the Section 87407 prohibit Director Tu from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to MWH Americas, Inc. ("MWH"), a consulting, engineering, and construction firm that 
specializes in infrastructure and water projects, with whom Director Tu's own consulting business 
is currently negotiating prospective employment? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit Director Tu' s consulting business from entering into a 
contract to provide outreach and public education services as a subcontractor to MWH (the 
"Subcontract") on MWH's contract with the City of San Diego to manage the City's Pure Water 
Program (the ''City Contract")?3. If Director Tu's consulting business enters into Subcontract with MWH, would Section 
I 090 prohibit her from making or participating in the making of, or MWDSC from entering into, 
future contracts with MWH?
sections.conclusion:
I. Yes. Section 87407 prohibits Director Tu from taking part in any decision directly 
relating to MWH because she is negotiating prospective employment with MWH. 
2. No. Section I 090 does not prohibit Director Tu's consulting business from entering into 
the Subcontract. 
3. Yes. If Director Tu's consulting business enters into the Subcontract, Section 1090 would 
prohibit her from making or participating in the making of, and MWDSC from entering into, future 
contracts with MWH because she would be financially interested in those future contracts.
sections.facts:
You are the General Counsel of the MWDSC and the authorized representative of Director 
Yen C. Tu. The Mayor of the City of San Diego, with the approval of the City Council, appointed 
Director Tu to represent the City on the San Diego County Water Authority ( .. SD8WA") Board of 
Directors pursuant to the San Diego County Water Authority Act. In her capacity as a SDCWA 
Director, Director Tu represents SDCWA on the MWDSC Board of Directors. Director Tu's role 
with the City does not include the administration, review, or approval of contracts entered into by 
the City. 
Last year, the City awarded a contract to manage its Pure Water Program to MWH, a 
consulting, engineering, and construction finn that specializes in infrastructure and water projects, 
and Brown and Caldwell, another consulting finn. As part of their contract with the City, MWH and 
Brown and Caldwell agreed to provide outreach and public education services for the project. 
Director Tu owns a consulting business in San Diego that specializes in marketing, 
community outreach, and conducting special events. Director Tu 's consulting business is currently 
negotiating the Subcontract with MWH to provide outreach and public education services on behalf 
of MWH on the City Contract. Director Tu's consulting fee under the Subcontract would be capped 
at $20,000 annually. The initial tenn of the Subcontract would end at the conclusion of the 2016 
calendar year, but the Subcontract would be renewable for four additional years for up to five years 
total. Director Tu has no ownership or other financial interest in MWH and would not participate in 
any profit sharing or receive financial benefits from the finn other than payments pursuant to the 
Subcontract. 
MWH is one of only eight firms of its type currently offering services related to MWDSC's 
mission and has a long history of contracting with MWDSC. MWH currently has seven active 
contracts for engineering, design, planning, and related services with MWDSC. Although there are 
no pending amendments to the current contracts or new contracts, existing contracts may be 
modified, and MWDSC will continue to have the opportunity and need to enter into new contracts  
with MWH, in the foreseeable future. According to its website, MWH is comprised of 
approximately 7,000 contractors and employees.
sections.analysis:
Section 87407 
We first consider whether Section 87407 prohibits Director Tu from taking part in any 
governmental decision directly relating to MWH. Section 87407 is designed to ensure that a public 
official does not use his or her position to make any governmental decisions that unduly benefit a 
prospective private sector employer. Section 87407 provides as follows: 
"No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her 
official position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to 
any person with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement 
concerning, prospective employment." 
Thus, once a public official has negotiated prospective employment or has made an arrangement 
regarding prospective employment, Section 87407 prohibits the official from making, participating 
in making, or using his or her official position to influence any governmental decision directly 
relating to the prospective employer.2 
For purposes of Section 87407, an official is "negotiating" employment when he or she 
interviews or discusses an offer of employment with an employer; has an "arrangement" concerning 
prospective employment when he or she accepts an employer's offer of employment; and is not 
"negotiating" or does not have an "arrangement" concerning prospective employment ifhe or she 
rejects or is rejected for employment. (Regulation 1874 7( c)( 1 )-(3 ).) 
Director Tu is negotiating prospective employment with MWH because she has discussed 
the Subcontract, an offer for contract employment with MWH. She would have an arrangement 
concerning prospective employment with MWH if she accepts MWH's offer and enters into the 
Subcontract. However, she would not be negotiating prospective employment with MWH if she 
rejects, or MWH retracts its offer of, the Subcontract. (Regulation 1874 7( c)(3 ).) 
Because Director Tu is negotiating prospective contract employment with MWH, Section 
87407 prohibits her from taking part in any decision directly relating to MWH, unless she rejects or 
is rejected for the contract employment with MWH. 
2 You have also asked whether prospective contract employment constitutes "prospective employment" for 
purposes of Section 87407. We have previously advised that Section 87407 prohibits a public official that negotiates 
contract work with a prospective employer from taking pan in any governmental decision directly relating to the 
prospective employer. (Culp Advice Lener, No. I-14-051; Hildreth Advice Letter, No.A-11-203; see Rctbamacl Advice 
Lener, No. A-I 0-030.) Therefore, consistent with our previous ad\'ice, we conclude that prospective contract 
employment constitutes "prospective employment" for purposes of Section 87407. 


Section 10903 
File No. A- 16-086  
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities from 
making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with financial 
interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising 
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigal v. 
City of Taft ( 1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual 
impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey 
(1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig ( 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the tenns of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.) When an officer with a proscribed financial interest is a member of the governing body of a 
public entity, the prohibition of Section I 090 extends to the entire body. (89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49, 
50 (2006); see Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. CozmǬv of Del Norte ( 1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201, 2 11-
212.) 
We employ the following analysis to determine whether an official has a conflict of interest 
under Section 1090. 
Step One: Is Director Tu subject to the provisions of Section 1090? 
Section I 090 provides in pertinent part as follows: "Members of the Legislature, state, 
county, district, judicial district and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in 
any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by anybody or board of which they are 
members." 
Director Tu is appointed by the Mayor of the City of San Diego with City Council approval 
to serve on the SDCW A Board, and in her capacity as a SDCW A Director, she represents SDCW A 
on the MWDSC Board. Director Tu's duties in her role as a City representative to the SDCW A 
Board do not include the administration, review, or approval of contracts entered into by the City. 
In each of these capacities with each of these agencies, the director is a public official for purposes 
of Section 1090. Therefore, Director Tu is subject to Section 1090. 
iWe do not analyze whether Section 87100 of the Act would prohibit Director Tu from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to future contracts with MWH if her consulting business enters into the Subcontract 
because Section 1090 would apply and would prohibit her from making or participating in the making of those future 
contracts. Note that Section 87100 would also prohibit Director Tu from making, participating in making, or using her 
official position to influence a governmental decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on her financial interest in MWH as a source of income 1f Section I 090 is not applicable.  
Steps Two and Three: Do the decisions at issue involve a contract that Director Tu will make 
in her official capacity? 
The Subcontract 
The decision at issue is whether MWH should enter into the Subcontract with Director Tu's 
consulting business. 
The Subcontract is between two private entities, MWH and Director Tu's consulting 
business. Furthennore, there is no indication from the facts presented that Director Tu "made or 
participated in the making" of the Subcontract in her capacity as a public official, or that she 
exerted any improper influence over the fonnation of the Subcontract in any way. Because Director 
Tu did not "make or participate in the making" of the Subcontract in her official capacity, we 
conclude that Section 1090 does not prohibit Director Tu's consulting business from entering into 
the Subcontract and do not further analyze this contract. 
Future Contracts with MWH 
Does the decision at issue involve a contract? 
The decision at issue is whether MWDSC ·may enter into potential future contracts with 
MWH if Director Tu' s consulting business enters into the Subcontract. 
To detennine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 34, 36 (2011 ); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 
( 1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and l 097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
'contract."' (Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569-571.) A decision to modify, 
extend, or renegotiate a contract constitutes involvement in the making of a contract under Section 
l 090. ( City of Imperial Beach, supra, at pp. 167-197.) 
MWH currently has seven active contracts for engineering, design, planning, and related 
services with MWDSC. Although there are no pending amendments to the current contracts or new 
contracts, you state that those existing contracts may be amended or modified, and MWDSC will 
continue to have the opportunity and need to enter into new contracts with MWH, in the foreseeable 
future. Thus, a decision relating to MWDSC's adoption of a new contract with MWH or its 
modification, extension, or renegotiation of an existing contract between them would involve a 
contract within the meaning of Section l 090. 
Would Director Tu be making or participating in the making of the potential future contracts 
between MWDSC and MWH? 
Where an official is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the 
contract at issue, he or she is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her 
agency's contracts irrespective of whether he or she actually participates in the making of the  
contract. (Thomson, supra, 38 Cal.3d 633, 649; Stigall, supra, 58 Cal.2d 565, 570-571; Fraser­
Yamor Agency, Inc. , .. County of Del Norte, supra. at pp. 21 1-212; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 49 (2006).) 
Thus, Director Tu would be presumed to be involved in the making of any potential future 
contract between MWDSC and MWH entered into while she is a member of the MWDSC Board 
because she is a member of the MWDSC Board. 
Step Four: \Vould Director Tu have a financial interest in potential future contracts between 
M\VDSC and M\VH? 
You assert that Director Tu would not be "financially interested" in potential future 
contracts between MWDSC and MWH if her consulting business enters into the potential 
Subcontract with MWH pursuant to Eden Township Healthcare Dist. v. Sutter Health (201 1 )  202 
Cal.App.4th 208. We view the holding from Eden more narrowly. 
In Eden, a district official participated in negotiating public contracts with a nonprofit 
corporation that employed the official as its president and chief executive officer. (Id. at pp. 2 1 2-
214.) The official had no ownership interest in the nonprofit corporation. (Ibid.) The appellate court 
found that the official was not financially interested those contracts because there was no evidence 
to suggest that the official's compensation would in any way be impacted by those contracts. (Id. at 
p. 227.) 
The facts presented are distinguishable from those in Eden. Director Tu is not a salaried 
employee of a nonprofit organization, she is the principal of her own for-profit consulting firm. The 
potential Subcontract between MWH and Director Tu's consulting business would confer a direct 
personal benefit to Director Tu, her annual consulting fee of up to $20,000, and would be renewable 
for four additional years beyond its initial one-year term for up to five years total. Director Tu's 
ongoing compensation from MWH under the Subcontract could be impacted by the potential future 
contracts between MWDSC and MWH because MWH could decide not to renew the Subcontract if 
Director Tu does not effectively advocate for MWH in her role as a MWDSC Director with respect 
to the potential future contracts. Therefore, Eden is not applicable to the facts presented. 
Section l 090 attempts to prevent honest government agents from succumbing to temptation 
by making it illegal for them to enter into relationships which are fraught with temptation. 
(Thomson, supra, at p. 648 citing Stigall, supra, at p. 570.) "The defining characteristic of a 
prohibited financial interest is whether it has the potential to divide an official's loyalties and 
compromise the undivided representation of the public interests the official is charged with 
protecting." (Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1075.) 
If Director Tu's consulting business enters into the Subcontract, she will be employed by 
MWH, and her ongoing employment under the Subcontract beyond its initial one-year term would 
be dependent on MWH's renewal of the Subcontract. In other words, in order to ensure the renewal 
of the Subcontract, Director Tu would be reliant on MWH. Accordingly, we find that Director Tu 
would be financially interested for purposes of Section 1090 in the potential future contracts 
between MWDSC and MWH if her consulting business enters into the Subcontract. 


Step Five: Does either a remote interest or non-interest exception apply?  
The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1 090's prohibition where 
the financial interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a 
"noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. 
If a "remote interest'' is present, the contract may be made if ( 1) the officer in question 
discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the body or board of which the officer is a 
member, (2) the interest is noted in the body or board's official records, and (3) the officer abstains 
from any participation in the body or board's making of the contract. (Section I 091 (a); 88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 1 06, 1 08 (2005); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).) If a "noninterest" is 
present, the contract may be made without the officer's abstention, and generally, a noninterest does 
not require disclosure. (City of Vernon v. Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 
508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (200 I ).) 
Reviewing the statutory exceptions, it appears that three "remote interest" exceptions, 
identified below, may apply to Director Tu. However, it does not appear that any of the nonintercst 
exceptions apply to Director Tu. 
We now consider the applicability of these remote interest exceptions to the facts presented. 
Section I 09 l(b)(2) 
The first exception under consideration is found in Section 1 09l (b)(2), which provides that 
there is a "remote interest" when: (I) the private contracting party has IO  or more employees other 
than the officer; (2) the officer was employed by the private contracting party at least three years 
prior to initially joining the public body; (3) the officer owns less than 3% of the stock in the private 
contracting party; (4) the officer is not an officer or director of the private contracting party; and (5) 
the officer did not directly participate in formulating the bid of the private contracting party. 
Based on the facts presented, Director Tu has not been employed by MWH for at least three 
years prior to initially joining the MWDSC Board. Therefore, this remote interest exception is not 
applicable. 
Section I 091 (b )(3) 
The exception set forth in Section 1091 (b )(3) provides that there is a "remote interest" 
when: ( 1) the agency with which the officer is a local public agency located in a county with a 
population ofless than 4,000,000; (2) the contract is competitively bid and is not for personal 
services; (3) the officer is not in a primary management capacity of the contracting party and holds 
no ownership interest in the contracting party; ( 4) the contracting party has IO or more other 
employees; (5) the employee or agent did not directly participate in formulating the bid of the 
contracting party; and (6) the contracting party is the lowest responsible bidder. 
MWDSC's headquarters is located in the City of Los Angeles within the County of Los 
Angeles, and the County's population is in excess of 10,000,000. Moreover, the potential 
Subcontract between MWH and Director Tu's consulting business is for the provision of outreach  
and public education services for the City of San Diego's Pure Water Program, and these services 
are personal services. Therefore, this remote interest does not apply. 
Section 1 09 l(b )(8) 
The third exception, contained in Section I 091 (b )(8), provides that there is a "remote 
interest" when the public officer has been a supplier of goods or services to the contracting party for 
at least five years prior to his or her election or appointment to office. 
Director Tu has not been employed by MWH for at least five years prior to initially joining 
the MWDSC Board. Therefore, this remote interest is not applicable. 
Step Six: Docs the rule of necessity apply'! 
In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a 
contract that Section I 090 would otherwise prohibit. (88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 1 10 (2005).) In 
procurement situations, the rule of necessity has permitted a government agency to acquire an 
essential supply or service despite a conflict of interest when the contracting officer would be the 
sole source of supply of the essential supply or service available. (65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 3 1 0  
( 1 982).) The rule of necessity has been found applicable only "in extreme cases of emergency, or 
where no alternative source of supply of goods or services existed." (59 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 604, 6 1 9, 
fn. 18 (1976).) 
You have provided no facts to suggest the "rule of necessity" would apply in the present 
situation. 
Thus, we conclude that if Director Tu' s consulting business enters into the Subcontract with 
MWH, Section 1090 would prohibit her from making or participating in the making of, and 
MWDSC from entering into, potential future contracts with MWH.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does the Section 87407 prohibit Director Tu from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to MWH Americas, Inc. ("MWH"), a consulting, engineering, and construction firm that 
specializes in infrastructure and water projects, with whom Director Tu's own consulting business 
is currently negotiating prospective employment? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit Director Tu' s consulting business from entering into a 
contract to provide outreach and public education services as a subcontractor to MWH (the 
"Subcontract") on MWH's contract with the City of San Diego to manage the City's Pure Water 
Program (the ''City Contract")?3. If Director Tu's consulting business enters into Subcontract with MWH, would Section 
I 090 prohibit her from making or participating in the making of, or MWDSC from entering into, 
future contracts with MWH?

CONCLUSION: I. Yes. Section 87407 prohibits Director Tu from taking part in any decision directly 
relating to MWH because she is negotiating prospective employment with MWH. 
2. No. Section I 090 does not prohibit Director Tu's consulting business from entering into 
the Subcontract. 
3. Yes. If Director Tu's consulting business enters into the Subcontract, Section 1090 would 
prohibit her from making or participating in the making of, and MWDSC from entering into, future 
contracts with MWH because she would be financially interested in those future contracts.

Scully General Counsel June 22, 2016 Metropolitan Waler District of Southern California Box 54153 Los Angeles. CA 90054-0153 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-086 Dear Ms. Scully: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Yen C. Tu, a member of the Board of Directors of the Metropolitan Water District of Southern California ("MWDSC"), regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090 et seq. 1 Please note that we do not advise on any other area oflaw, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. When a request for advice involves potential issues raised under Section I 090, the Commission is required to forward a copy of the request to the Attorney General's Office and the local district attorney prior to proceeding with the advice. (Section 1097 .1 ( c)(3 ). ) Accordingly, we have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office. We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) Finally, the following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requester. (Section 1097.1 (c)(5).) QUESTIONS 1. Does the Section 87407 prohibit Director Tu from taking part in governmental decisions relating to MWH Americas, Inc. ("MWH"), a consulting, engineering, and construction firm that specializes in infrastructure and water projects, with whom Director Tu's own consulting business is currently negotiating prospective employment? 2. Does Section 1090 prohibit Director Tu' s consulting business from entering into a contract to provide outreach and public education services as a subcontractor to MWH (the "Subcontract") on MWH's contract with the City of San Diego to manage the City's Pure Water Program (the ''City Contract")? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 910l4. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 oflhe California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-16-086 Page No. 2 3. If Director Tu's consulting business enters into Subcontract with MWH, would Section I 090 prohibit her from making or participating in the making of, or MWDSC from entering into, future contracts with MWH? CONCLUSIONS I. Yes. Section 87407 prohibits Director Tu from taking part in any decision directly relating to MWH because she is negotiating prospective employment with MWH. 2. No. Section I 090 does not prohibit Director Tu's consulting business from entering into the Subcontract.
